abstract: The catalytic conversion of CO2 into renewable fuels like methanol is a promising strategy for climate change mitigation, yet it is often limited by the chemical inertness of the CO2 molecule. While bulk gold is catalytically inactive for hydrogenation, this study demonstrates that gold nanoparticles supported on a CeOx/TiO2(110) interface exhibit remarkable activity and selectivity for methanol synthesis at exceptionally low pressures. Using a combination of ambient-pressure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (AP-XPS), scanning tunneling microscopy (STM), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we show that the addition of ceria nanoclusters to a titania support significantly enhances the dispersion of gold and creates highly active interfacial sites. These sites facilitate a unique electronic metal-support interaction, characterized by local charge redistribution that polarizes the Au nanoparticles and stabilizes key intermediates such as carboxylates and formates. DFT results indicate that the Ce3+ species in the mixed oxide support drastically lower the energy barrier for CO2 activation and the subsequent hydrogenation steps compared to Au/TiO2. These findings reveal that the synergistic interaction between gold and a reducible mixed-oxide interface can transform an inert noble metal into a highly effective catalyst, providing a new pathway for the low-pressure hydrogenation of CO2 to methanol.
